medications.indication_rationale: Benign ventricular ectopy triggered by caffeine and fatigue does not require pharmacological treatment; management is trigger reduction and reassurance. No medication was prescribed in the source note.

Beta-blockers may occasionally be considered for troublesome symptomatic ectopy persisting despite trigger modification, but this is a GP/specialist decision after ECG review, not an appropriate first-line telehealth recommendation for a first presentation with clear reversible triggers.
contraindications_in_this_case: Not appropriate as an initial telehealth recommendation before trigger modification is trialled and a baseline ECG is obtained

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop further coffee/caffeine today and substantially reduce intake over the coming days (for example, to no more than one cup in the morning).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Excess caffeine is a well-recognised, reversible precipitant of ventricular ectopy; reducing intake is the single most impactful modifiable factor identified in this case.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use a slow-breathing technique (for example the 4-7-8 pattern: inhale 4 seconds, hold 7, exhale 8) when lying in bed to increase vagal tone and reduce the thudding sensation, allowing restful sleep.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Slow breathing raises vagal tone and can suppress the subjective prominence of ectopic beats; it also directly addresses the acute anxiety. This technique is drawn from the source note's own care plan.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Prioritise rest and adequate sleep and reduce excessive working hours over the coming days.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Fatigue and sleep deprivation increase sympathetic tone and symptom awareness; improving rest is likely to reduce recurrence.

patient_education_points.point: I can hear how frightening this is, especially on your own at night — that's completely understandable when your heart feels like it's stopping. The good news is that what you're describing, a skipped beat followed by a strong thud, along with everything you've told me, points to extra heartbeats (ectopics), which are very common and not dangerous in someone with no other symptoms.

What's happening is that a cell in the lower chamber of your heart is occasionally firing early. Your heart pauses briefly to reset, and the next beat is stronger because it has had a little more time to fill — that's the heavy 'thud' you feel. It is not your heart failing or stopping.

The heavy coffee today, plus the long hours and exhaustion, are very likely triggers — cutting back on caffeine and getting some proper rest should reduce how often this happens. Slow, deep breathing (like the 4-7-8 pattern) when you lie down can also settle the sensation.

It's still worth getting a baseline ECG and some routine blood tests with your GP within the next week or so, just to formally document your heart's rhythm and rule out simple things like a low potassium level — this isn't urgent, but it's worth having on record.

If your heart suddenly starts racing fast and doesn't settle (for example more than 120 beats a minute for over 15 minutes), or you get central/crushing chest pain, severe shortness of breath, or you actually faint, call 000 straight away — but based on what you've told me tonight, none of those are present.
It's completely okay to feel anxious about this — if the worry about your heart keeps affecting your sleep or daily life even after this settles, it would be worth mentioning to your GP as well.

follow_up_plan.interval: Routine GP review within about 7 days for a baseline ECG and basic bloods, with a Holter monitor considered if symptoms persist
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: 12-lead ECG rhythm documentation, serum electrolytes (including potassium), thyroid function tests, and reassessment of symptom frequency after caffeine reduction and improved rest; Holter monitor if symptoms persist
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Sustained rapid palpitations that don't settle, central/crushing chest pain, severe breathlessness, or fainting — call 000 rather than waiting for the routine appointment

must_include_items: Explicit validation/empathetic acknowledgement of the patient's fear and distress (alone at night, fear the heart is failing)
Clear reassurance that the described symptoms, combined with the reassuring history elicited, do not indicate an emergency, without dismissing the concern outright
Identification of heavy caffeine and fatigue/poor sleep as likely reversible triggers, with specific behavioural advice to reduce them
Recommendation for a routine (non-urgent) ECG and basic bloods rather than no follow-up at all
Safety-netting: call 000 for sustained rapid palpitations, central/crushing chest pain, severe breathlessness, or fainting
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending the routine ECG be arranged the same week rather than exactly 7 days is acceptable, provided it is framed as routine rather than urgent/emergency
Offering slow-breathing/relaxation guidance and anxiety-management resources in addition to the cardiac reassurance is an acceptable and valuable addition
errors_of_omission: No acknowledgement of the patient's emotional distress
No identification of caffeine/fatigue as likely triggers
No follow-up ECG or investigation recommended at all
No safety-netting for red flag symptoms provided
errors_of_commission: Recommending emergency department attendance or ambulance call despite the reassuring history and absence of red flags (over-triage)
Dismissing the patient's concern without any empathetic acknowledgement ('it's nothing, don't worry about it')
Prescribing a beta-blocker or other cardiac medication via telehealth without ECG review or specialist input
Recommending an urgent 24-hour Holter monitor or urgent cardiology referral as an immediate next step, which over-investigates a reassuring first presentation
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a true-negative/reassurance case. Both under-reacting (no follow-up plan, dismissive tone) and over-reacting (emergency escalation, urgent Holter/cardiology referral) are scoring failures. The correct response calibrates to the actual clinical risk while validating the patient's distress and retaining clear red-flag safety-netting.
